Muhammad Aksa Ali (Student’s signature) 3 1 .0 5 .2023 (Date) INTRODUCTION As the  whole world is transforming and people are constantly changing,  so are  their values.   “People develop values based on their heritage and life experiences, and those values in turn influence subsequent  behaviors” ( Kahle et al.,1970) .   Moreover, these values may also change with time or when “any of the cultural, environmental or social situations to which people must adapt, change” (Kahle, 2007) .  When consumers have to choose a certain brand, they look at their values and the attitudes stemming from these values. Thus, in the decision-making process,  when a customer is buying a certain product or service,  the attitudes and behaviors exhibited are influenced by their values (Homer, Pamela & Kahle, Lynn, 1988).  Therefore, it is important for companies to identify the values which hold the most significance for their customers when designing their   strategies  to optimize the value chain . Through this, the brand may deliver on customers’ expectations, driving up the brand loyalty, brand image, customer satisfaction and encourage repeat purchase ( Lindgreen  &  Wynstra , 2005). It is especially crucial in the fashion industry due to  its  fast-paced nature. Thus, for companies to stay relevant, they have to adapt their  business  strategies for then to collide with customers’ needs and values. Pakistan is home to approximately 23 3  million people  ( Worldometer , 2023) , whose values have been changing with time , causing fashion companies to change their approach. A significant proportion of these people is a part of the fashion market with their purchase of apparel, the revenue for which amounted to nearly US $ 6 billion as of 2023  (Statista, 2023).  As the customer’s demand grows, the market observes expanding revenue. Therefore, it is more important now than ever for companies operating in the country to mend their approaches and strategies and therefore benefit from higher sales and profits. A s the c ustomer  is the core of all business activities,  their  behavior has utmost important and direct connection to the successful  implementation of  companies’   value chain stages,  activities and financial prosperity.  The  goal  of this paper is  the  provision of managerial implications  for fashion companies in Pakistan  in adapting their   value chain  to align with the customer values. By considering everything that has been mentioned before, this paper’s research outcomes would be significant and have managerial implications for many companies. The result s received from the research in this paper will be valuable for businesses in fashion industry, as the analysis of customer values will enable fashion companies in Pakistan to evaluate their risks and opportunities and therefore adapt their  value chain.   The research  objectives  are:   To identify customer values term and typology To identify the influence of customer values on business value chain stages To identify trends in customer values, their reasons and analyse the impact of COVID-19 on them To characterise Pakistan’s fashion market To explore the current business value chain employed by fashion companies in Pakistan To determine the key customer values with use of a survey among customers of fashion companies in Pakistan To provide  managerial implications  to fashion companies in Pakistan for adapting their value chain to align with the customer values The research  object  of this paper is the fashion companies operating in Pakistan. The research  subject  is the  value chain of  fashion companies in Pakistan.  The thesis paper is based on two types of information: primary and secondary sources. In primary sources,  questionnaire for the  customers of fashion companies  in Pakistan and  interviews of fashion companies in Pakistan  are used . The list of interview questions is provided in the Appendix and the result obtained i s  mentioned in the pertinent chapter of this paper. The goal  of questionnaire is to gain more  insights and information about how the trends in customer values  and of interviews is to assess the  impact  on  specific stages of value chain  of the  fashion  companies  in Pakistan.   In case of secondary sources, desk research was extensively utilized.  Scientific publications, professional and newspaper articles, journal articles, websites, textbooks and books were used. This information was found from databases such as EBSCO, JSTOR, Elsevier and the GSOM library.  The thesis paper consists of t hree  chapters, where the  1 st  one is focused on defining the key types of customer values , analyzing the ir influence on business value chain,  identifying the key trends in customer values ,  the ir  reasons   and influence of COVID-19 on them. The  2 nd   chapter  examin es  Pakistan’s fashion industry, including the key features and market characteristics , with interview of three prominent fashion companies in Pakistan  to gain perspective about the existing value chain.  In  3 rd  chapter , the customer values in Pakistan’s fashion industry were determined  through a questionnaire and  in the end  managerial implications were provided for the fashion companies in Pakistan for aligning their value  chain with the customer values identified. C HAPTER  1:  Customer values: major characteristics, trends and their relation to business value chain 1 .1   Customer  v alue: A comprehensive overview 1.1.1 Defini ng  c ustomer  va lue The first part of this paper is devoted to the assessment  of  customer s  values and identification of the ir  main trends for use in the subsequent chapter  devoted  to the  value chain  of fashion businesses.   Now, more than ever, fashion companies globally are concerned with  researching and identifying  the value of their product s   and  service s  for customers in order to provide them the value proposition  that  matches their requirements. As the business strategies based on delivering value to customers provides brands with a greater return o n  their invested resources and therefore profits, more companies are working on accurately understanding what the customer values are and how they  are changing.  Therefore, from the outset,  it is important to  identif y what is value and subsequently, what are  customer values . In the framework of business and marketing,  there are several definitions of the term ‘value’ offered by various authors. According to Anderson and Narus:  “ Value in business markets is the worth in monetary terms of the technical, economic, service, and social benefits a customer receives in exchange for the price it pays for a market offering. ”   A more concise definition is offered by Schechter: Value is “all factors, both qualitative and quantitative, subjective and objective, that make up the complete shopping experience”  ( Babin  et al., 1994).  As value is a concept which has not  been  defined or measured to a great extent in the academic literature, basing on the available work and above stated definitions,  the following definition  is proposed and will be the basis of this paper .  “Value is the utility of a product or service for the customer which he/she receives through their customer journey” (Author of the paper).  Furthermore, it is now important to define c ustomer value . Also  known as customer perceived value and customer received value,   it  is the “relativistic preference characterizing a subject’s experience of interacting with some object i.e., any good, service  etc. ” (Holbrook, 1994).  Moreover, from another perspective it is the “consumer’s perception of net benefits gained in exchange for the costs incurred in obtaining the desired benefits” (Chen & Dubinsky, 2003).   In order to summarize these perspectives, this paper employs the definition proposed by  Paanen  & Seppänen  (2013)  in their review of customer value literature which stated that “ The core logic of customer value revolves around understanding and capturing customer expectations, creating and delivering desired customer experiences, and assessing and managing the customer evaluation ”. 1.1.2 Typology  of  c ustomer  v alue A number of approaches exist for identification of values and their typology. In this paper, two of them are considered. The first categorization of customer values is done by  Almquist , Senior and  Boch  (2016), who identified a collection of 30 elements of value and categorized them into  functional, emotional, life changing and social impact . These values were illustrated based  on the concept of Abraham Maslow’s renowned hierarchy of  needs  in figure below by Bain & Company.    Fig  1.1 . 1. :   Categorization of values as per hierarchy of needs Source:  Bain & Company  Inc,  HBR (2016) The pertinence of the se  values varies in different industries, cultures and demographics. The model  presented  is a heuristic one  i.e.,  it is practical and the most powerful values are at the top. For a company to provide its customers with higher order elements, at first some of the functional elements have to be  provided, similar  to the hierarchy of needs.  Additionally , certain elements such as quality, which is part of the functional category, w ere  shown to matter more than others. The  customers  require the products or services to attain a certain minimal level of quality, and other elements can’t make  amends for a crucial lack of it. After the quality, the critical element depends on the industry. In apparel retail  and fashion businesses , variety, avoidance of hassles, design/aesthetics and saving time are the elements which matter the most  for customers . Moreover, digital firms are perceived as providers of more value. As the interactions of consumers are made easier and more convenient, the  well-designed  digital companies excel in elements ‘saves time’ and ‘avoids  hassles ’ . Customers scored Zappos , an American online fashion retailer,  higher than its traditional apparel competitor on these elements.  The second   categorization  of customer values which will be used extensively in this paper is proposed by Smith  & Colgate  in ‘The Journal of Marketing Theory and Practice’,  presented in Fig. 1.1.2,  is an amalgamation and extension of major works on customer value creation . Fig  1. 1.2 :   Major types of value Source:   J. Brock Smith and Mark Colgate (Made by author) There exist 4 major types of values, each one of them having key facets as outlined in the  figure above .  Functional value: Functional or instrumental value focuses on the extent to which a product or service has the appropriate characteristics, is useful for the customer and performs the function. The main facets are: correct attributes, features and characteristics ( which includes  e.g.,  quality,  customization  values ), appropriate performances ( e.g.,  reliability, performance, service support outcomes), and appropriate outcomes ( e.g.,  effectiveness, operational and environmental benefits). Companies such as Ford and  Sony focus on performance. On the other hand, pharmaceuticals like Pfizer focus on outcomes for customers.   Thus, for the purpose of this research, the following functional values will be the primary focus:   quality, variety, customization, convenience and simplification, information availability, after-sale and support services.  Experiential value: Experiential, also known as hedonic value, is concerned with how product produces experiences and feelings pertinent to customers. Sensory values ( e.g.,  ambiance, aroma) are used by restaurants and certain retailers; emotional values ( e.g.,  enjoyment, excitement, humor) by travel and entertainment companies; social/ relational values ( e.g.,  network, connectedness, trust development and commitment) practiced by professional service organizations; and lastly travel and hotel corporations use epistemic value ( e.g.,  curiosity, novelty).  For experiential values, major one’s this paper will focus on are: sensory, entertainment, novelty, wellness, authenticity, sustainability and social media networks.  Symbolic value: Symbolic or expressive value assesses the extent to which customers associate a product with a psychological meaning. Customer’s  self-identity  or worth may be linked to some products  e.g.,  luxury goods, some other products such as music or vacations have personal meaning or  associations.  Moreover, customers may use products for self-expression ( e.g.,  through perfumes), social meaning ( e.g.,  owning prestigious brands) or conditional meaning, which relate to socio-cultural or ethnic traditions.  In the symbolic values, the focus will be on self-identity and social meaning values. Cost value: Although customers wish to maximize their  values, they  also wish to minimize the costs and other aspects, wishing to attain cost/sacrifice value. These costs are: economic costs which include product price and opportunity cost; the psychological  e.g.,  convenience, search costs; the personal investment  e.g.,  time, effort, energy used to purchase and consume and risk  e.g.,  personal, financial in owning and using the product by using guarantees, flexible return etc.  For cost values of customers, economic costs, psychological and personal investments and risks are highlighted and used further on.  The  first categorization by  Almquist  et al. identifies 30 broad elements of value and are presented in form of a hierarchy, which may vary for different customers depending on their culture,  demographical features, and may matter more to some, than the other, depending on where they stand on this scale. Whereas, the second one by Smith & Colgate , takes a different approach, whereby it identifies three value types associated with the benefits acquired by customer and one of them with the cost incurred, therefore the values from this typology may be applicable to a larger customer group. Therefore, considering this, the second typology will be taken  as the basis of key customer values discussion further on in the paper  as it is more instrumental  for this  research. Nonetheless, some parts of the first categorization in Figure 1 .1.1  will still be used to explain customer values  further on.  Having elucidated the types of values, it is now possible to identify and describe  categories of each type of customer values in detail : Functional value s : Quality As per Peter Drucker, “Quality in a product or service is not what the supplier puts in; it is what the customer gets out and is willing to pay for. Customers pay only for what is of use to them and gives them value. Nothing else constitutes quality.”  HBR identifies some of the factors influencing consumer perception of quality are: brand name, previous experience, performance specifications, warranty, service repairs, spare parts availability and more (Takeuchi and Quelch, 1983). A few years later, David Garvin  (1987)  suggested that quality has 8 dimensions, highlighted in the Fig  1.1.3 : Variety The variety seeking of consumers is the ‘tendency of individuals to seek diversity in their choices of goods or services' (Kahn, 1995). McAlister  (1982)  noted if a customer consumes a certain product repeatedly, they may become satiated with the specific attributes and prefer to instead consume another one. As per the marginal utility theory, as consumption increases, the marginal utility derived from each extra unit decreases. Therefore, repeatedly consuming, or in this case purchasing, a product reduces its appeal.  Fig 1. 1. 3 . :   Dimensions of quality Source:   David Garvin (Made by author) There exist several internal and external factors influencing the variety seeking behavior of customers. Yuan Zhang  (2022)  identified the following in a comprehensive literature review. For internal factors, individual demographics such as age or gender. Individual personality such as chronically indecisiveness in customers leads to them selecting a mix of products (Jeong et al., 2016 ).   Moreover, customers with a growth mindset tend to be more variety seeking (Li and Sun, 2021). As per a study by Deng et al.  (2016) , customers are more prone to variety when products are horizontally displayed. Last but not the least, the mindset of clients plays a crucial role as well. The variety seeking is also affected by external factors, for instance social environment. Customers are more likely to choose variety in situation of social pressure as they desire to receive positive feedback from others (Ratner & Khan, 2002). Kahn  further  notes that price promotions prompted customers to choose variety and once these were retracted, they were again loyal to their favorite brands. The physical environment plays an equally important role. Finally, the implications of well-designed marketing strategies such as ‘offer framing effect’ have been examined, whereby customers seek greater variety when they are offered products in singles, rather than in bundles (Mittelman et al., 2014).  Customization Through customization, also referred to as personalization, products and services can be tailored to an individual’s specific needs and/or preferences (Tiihonen & Felfernig, 2017). This customization and personalization use the insight of each customer’s data including personal and behavioral and therefore delivers them with a superior experience. There is a customer desire to own a specific product which would carry their personal signature, and companies are acting on this, as the benefit to them is seen in form of 5-15%  (McKinsey, 2019)  increase in revenue directly through the implementation of personalization (Boudet et al., 2019).  Convenience and simplification Convenience is essential in the contemporary world. “Human beings are designed to generally choose the more convenient option when making intuitive decisions” (Forbes, 2020).  Whether it is the ease of receiving required information  or  completing a purchase online, all factors play an important role. Reduction of friction and frustration in customer’s journey creates ease. Nearly 50% of consumers are influenced by convenience more than price and some are willing to sacrifice saving on cost for convenience (Eclipse Group, 2021). The National Research Foundation discovered that as high as 97% of customers have backed out of purchasing a product or service due to inconvenience. Correspondingly, customers highlighted the factors they value the most in their customer service, results of which back this claim. On the Fi g 1.1. 4 ,  the  respondents  were asked  which of the factors is important and for which they are willing to pay more for. Expectedly, convenience, ease (in payment) and efficiency were on top of the list.  Fig 1. 1.4 :   Most important customer experience factors Source:   Puthiyamadam and  Reyes   ( 2017/18 )   Information availability Customers want to live their values through the products or services they buy. This has made them even more keen to know more about the product-how it was sourced, manufactured and delivered to them. It is also one of the factors that encourages consumers to buy more sustainable products for 20% of customers (IBM, 2022). The product information quality also minimizes the searching and processing costs for customers  (Park and Kim, 2003).   Moreover, it is often necessary for companies to integrate the information about how the product is made and its production cycle into their website (Forza and Salvador, 2006). After-sale and support service According to HBR’s research, acquiring new customers is 5-25 times more expensive than retaining an existing one  (Gallo, 2014).  Therefore, after-sales service is a key element for any business, aiming to retain customers who value it. Some examples of after-sale service sought by customers include help with installation process, maintenance of products, clear exchange and return policy, help desk services, real-time online support and automated customer service lines  (Chen, 2020).  Consumer perception of product quality is enhanced through after-sale services (Asugman et  al., 1997). Customer values and experience are holistic in nature and are created partially by elements outside of retailers control including after sale experience (Verhoef et al, 2009).  Experiential values: Sensory Senses play a crucial role in  customer’s  perception and influence th e  decision-making behavior. Sensory values include all 5 senses being leveraged and thus impact perception and memories  (Manzano et al., 2019).  Customer’s judgements about a brand or product/service are driven by: smells they encounter i.e., the olfactory system, things they hear i.e., auditory system, objects they touch i.e., tactile system, the tastes they experience i.e., gustatory system and finally what they see i.e. the visual system (Childers, 20 10 ). Ambient factors, in the likes of music and lighting and layout impact a customer’s behavior (Baker et al., 1994) /    Customers also feel that context of the product displayed is important. The retail display decisions and external cues are all observed when making a purchase decision (Buchanan et al., 1999). Back in the 1970s, Kotler had highlighted the dominance atmosphere has on the customer decision, more than the product itself.  Entertainment As per Michael J. Wol f (1999) , we are living in the age of the ‘entertainment economy’. Customers value being entertained, indulged, amused and surprised through their purchases and information they receive. This makes entertainment as one of the criteria of purchase decisions. The entertainment devices are diverse and it is common for different people in the same household to be immersed in distinct devices- a games console, a virtual reality headset, watching videos on smartphone and using social media on the laptop. As the new trends are setting into the market, people are looking for new opportunities to entertain themselves.  Novelty Novelty or innovation is an offering which is perceived as new by consumers within a market segment (Hoyer et al., 2017). Innovations and their three types create a different variety of change in customer’s consumption patterns: continuous innovations have limited impact, the dynamically continuous such as Smart watches may have a pronounced effect and discontinuous innovations are completely new and radically change consumer behavior, for instance internet service at its dawn. The  perceived value and benefits of novel products or services are criteria affecting consumers’ adoption decisions.  Wellness Nowadays, customers view wellness through a broader and more sophisticated lens, which includes not only fitness and nutritional value of products consumed, but also mental and physical health .  A survey by Forbes illustrated that 79% of respondents feel that wellness is important for them and for 42% it is a top priority. There are six broad dimensions of wellness, including: Better health Better fitness Better nutrition Better appearance Better sleep Better mindfulness  Overall, with it becoming an important value for customers, they are expected to increase their use of products and services which enhance their wellness. There exist several trends related to wellness which have been gaining momentum, such as natural/clean products in the form of skincare, vitamins, fitness products e.g., wearables being sold online, and services concentrating on mental health.   Authenticity Consumers aim to seek authenticity to find meaning in life and in relation to personal goals and objectives (Emmons, 2005) and so choose brands which reinforce the identity they desire. According to Rose and Wood’ s (2005) ,   the  notion of ‘hyper authenticity’ states that consumers actively construct personal useful notions of what is authenticity. Therefore, different personal goals enable customers to find the authenticity in different brands, products etc. which others may deem inauthentic (Beverland et al., 2010). Nowadays, statistically, while choosing a brand to support and be loyal to, 90% of customers are looking for authenticity  ( DeGruttola , 2019).  Customers can also easily recognize when a brand uses cause-marketing as a strategy for profit-making and do not wish to genuinely help a cause (Wertz, 2021). According to Hilary Hartling, a brand strategist, “consumers are discerning and want to know that the brands they choose share their values”.  Sustainability More customers nowadays are searching for brands which match their values. 6/10 customers are said to be willing to change their shopping habits to reduce the environmental footprint and 8/10 said that sustainability is an important value for them. Moreover, for those customers who feel sustainability is very important, 70% may consider paying surcharge for brands which are sustainable   (Haller  et al. , 2020) . Social media Social networking sites (SNS) are becoming major sources of information, through which customers may easily gain access to information regarding product information  (Silver and Matthews, 2016).  More customers nowadays are seeking advice from other users or peer customers of the product, service or brand itself  (Gan and Wang, 2017).  Customer experiences are becoming more social in nature and the vast majority of touchpoints are presented through social media (Lemon and Verhoef, 2016). Consequently, consumers are not passive users of information but active producers and distributors of information and content. They are also referring to social media to solve collective action problems. This includes the features ‘ratings’ and ‘prestige’. This is used on sites such as eBay, Amazon, TripAdvisor etc. where a standard model of star rating out of five and critical commentary may be provided (Abbott, 2012).  Symbolic values: Self-identity According to previous consumer research, people consume products or services which are consistent with their self-identity and perception. So, the “choice of product is influenced by consumers sense of self” (Townsend  and  Sood , 2012). Claude Steele’s theory of self-affirmation provides a psychological backing to this value. The notion is that people aim to affirm their personal values in order to feel themselves as complete individuals and portray to others in a positive light. The aesthetics as a product attribute, has the ability to affirm the self-identity, similar to brands which produce these products. Indeed, Escalas and Bettman’s  (2005)  research shows that consumers are known to construct and maintain their self-identity through use of branded products or services. Additionally, people are often assimilating or conforming their behavior to that of others, which is linked to receiving validation (Brewer, 1991).   Conversely, there also exists pressure for differentiation  due to the wish to be unique. In this case people will wish to purchase products which are scarcer. Chan et al . (2012)  highlight that consumers may show conformation behavior in a group with one choice and differentiation with another. Further to that, consumers in certain situations may have a more independent self-identity, i.e., do not link closely to their cultural, ethnic background, and thus are more likely to avoid identity-linked products. This may happen in situations when there is a social identity threat and the individual wishes to not be associated with a negatively viewed group (White et al., 2012). Social meaning Social meaning and in turn social status are characterized by contemporary culture with its primacy on expenditure on material things which establish social relationships. With 2020 marking a new high of $252 billion in aggregate luxury goods sold (Deloitte, 2021), it’s explicitly visible how customers preference is dominated by more expensive but functionally similar goods, or in other terms ‘conspicuous consumption’ as stated by Veble n (1925)  in his theory of leisure class. Customers in general regard wealth as desirable as it indicates that firstly, an individual possesses ample resources to purchase a luxurious good and secondly, ability to procure resources (Plourde, 2008) As has been denoted by many studies carried out so far, people tend to view people who display luxury goods more favorably than those who don't.  As customers know that luxury brands are reliable wealth indicators because they are more costly than non-luxury brands, they take this sign at its face value (Nielsen and Meijers, 2011).  Cost values:  Economic costs Product costs and opportunity cost are the key economic costs considered by a customer when making a decision of whether to buy or not. This consumers’ willingness to pay (WTP) impacts the price the customer pays i.e., the product costs. According to Cherne v (2012) , people use categorical reasoning i.e., they tend to classify products as expensive or inexpensive, which therefore influences how they see them and how much they are willing to pay. Furthermore, brand awareness, perceived quality, brand equity all have an effect on the product cost the customer is willing to incur.   On the other hand, opportunity cost, the foundation of economics, is what a customer accounts for in their purchase decisions. Whenever there is a resource constraint and limited use resources are in the picture, opportunity cost is considered. Therefore, “consumers who consider opportunity cost are  more sensitive to their value than those who don't” ( Sp iller, 2011).  Consumers nowadays are considering the cost they are likely to incur more than before, as they wish to gain the most out of their money spent. Psychological costs  and personal investment Psychological costs may be summarized through convenience costs, which are “incurred through expenditure of time, physical and nervous energy and money required to overcome the frictions of space and time to obtain possession of goods”(Kelley, 1958 ).   Nowadays, customers are putting greater emphasis on the psychological cost minimization and prefer commodity transactions which are more economically and psychologically more favorable for them. Research revealed that 7/10   customers believe convenience is more important for them now, as compared to previous years  (Marcom News, 2018).   Moreover, t he effort customers make in making a purchase may include the physical effort of going to the store, searching for a product and cognitively evaluating several options , which is all part of personal investment.  Additionally, the sunk costs are also taken into account i.e., the sunk effort when looking for a product in terms of time spent. Willingness to spend much time and effort to purchase has been decreasing among customers on one hand, however others believe that the amount of effort invested by a customer would positively impact the willingness to buy a product and how much they pay for it (Lala & Chakraborty, 2015). Risk Personal and financial risk determines the purchasing behavior of a customer. For instance, if a purchase turns out to be unpleasant and a failure, it is a possible financial risk and there would be doubt whether it is worth paying the given price for or whether it can be bought at a lower price point. Moreover, there is a performance risk where the consumer may be concerned if the product will serve its initial purchase purpose and meet all expectations. These and other risks occur during customers' process of selecting a good or service. Post pandemic, over ⅓ of the customers are becoming more risk averse in terms of financial risk  (Tomes, 2021) .   The customer values have been illustrated according to their types in  the  F i g 1.1. 5  as a summary of aforementioned information. Fig.1. 1.5 :   Typology of customer values and its components Source:   Made by author , b ased on  typologies of  Smith & Colgate and  Almquist  et al.     After h aving  categorized  the types of values,  now the influence of these customer values on the business value chain and its stages will be considered in detail.  1.2 Customer values influence on business value chain’s stages Companies globally are increasingly focusing on their strategic planning activities, in order to make effective decisions regarding their competitive position and  therefore increase profitability (Ensign, 2001). In order to carry this out, value chain analysis is used for a linkage to be created between different activities of the business which create value for the customer.  The term ‘value chain’ was coined by Michael Porter in 1985,  which is “a systematic way of examining all the activities a firm performs and how they interact is necessary for analyzing the sources of competitive advantage.” And “A firm gains competitive advantage by performing these strategically important activities more cheaply or better than its competitors”.   This will be the basis of further work as it is the most comprehensive  definition. Furthermore, buyer driven chains will be considered as the focus of the paper is on customers. Buyer-driven chains are more common in the “labor-intensive and consumer goods industries in which large retailers play an important role in production networks establishment ( Abecassis - Moedas, 2006). An important aspect in value chains is that of value addition, which implies value creation and capture for the customer. Every activity done by business  should add value ( Chivaka , 2007). Therefore, the whole idea of value creation is the ability of firm to deliver high performance and benefit for customers ( Kothandaraman  and Wilson, 2001). All in all, as per Porter, just reducing the costs is not enough and in order to win customers and for a firm to stay competitive on the market, customer’s opinions, wants and preferences should be the primary goal of business, and value chain helps do exactly this by being different from competitors, therefore gaining a competitive advantage.  M. Porter’s value chain model was chosen as the framework in this work to analyze the customer values   as it is the most researched and well-established one. It  is  the most comprehensive model which illustrates distinctly how value is created through all the business activities, and therefore is delivered to customers. Furthermore, this framework aligns closely to the customer values, the point focal in this work. Thus, it is significant in analyzing how the value for customers is created and delivered to them.  The value chain devised by Porter is presented in Fi g. 1.2.1 . , which  involves the breakdown of all firm’s activities into 5 primary value activities and 4 support or secondary value activities, after which all of them are analyzed in order to identify the ones which provide greater competitive advantage for the firm , and in the end generate margin.  In order to define what each of them entails , table  1.2. 2 .  has been created to summarize all the relevant activities .  Fig. 1. 2 .1:   Porter’s Value Chain Source:   ‘ Competitive  advantage’ by Porter, 1985, p. 37    Table 1.2. 2 :  Summary of activities in value chain Primary activities Inbound logistics Receiving, storing, material handling, inventory control etc.  Operations Transforming the inputs or raw materials into the finished good, by using, for instance packaging, assembly, testing, printing etc.   Outbound logistics Collecting, storing, distributing products; warehousing, order processing etc. Marketing and sales Advertisement, promotion, pricing, quotation, communication etc. Service Enhance or maintain value with installation, repair, supply of parts, adjustment etc. Secondary (support) activities Firm infrastructure General management, finance, accounting, planning, quality management, legal, governmental activities Technology development Equipment, software, hardware, know-hows Human resource development Recruiting, hiring, training, compensation, development Procurement Acquisition of inputs (machinery, equipment, buildings) and resources Source:   ‘ Competitive  advantage’ by Porter, 1985  (made by author)   Having defined the value chain and the stages of Porter’s framework, it is now possible to assess the influence of the customer values on different stages of the value chain. This process would enable the correct alignment of types of customer values to the stages in order to understand which stages are important for customers and for firms in decision making and prioritize their actions in order to create higher brand loyalty and customer satisfaction. For this, the customer values identified  previously will be used and therefore linked to each stage of the value chain of M. Porter.  In several cases, the values are linked to more than one stage. Primary activities: Inbound logistics Quality The  effectiveness  of inbound logistics  would ensure that in the production of goods, high quality materials are used in order for a high-quality product to be delivered to customers, therefore satisfying their demands.  In case quality standards are not maintained and there are certain defects in the product due to the poor  storage or material handling, there may be negative impact on this customer value, causing dissatisfaction. Problems in a company’s inventory management are a sign for the company to introduce systems for improving these processes e.g., JIT supplies ( Zimon , 2019).  Sustainability Sustainability has been becoming very important in the companies, including the inbound logistics, as there may be a significant impact on the whole value chain. It is related to several aspects such as firstly the sourcing of raw materials.  Since mid 2020, companies are moving to secure supplies of green resources and low emission resources through decarbonizing suppliers’ energy use, redesigning products to use different material, partner with suppliers to implement low emission processes etc. (McKinsey, 2022). Through incorporating these practices, businesses enable the customers environmental and ethical concerns to be addressed. Operations Quality Quality is likewise related to the operations stage in the value chain. Through this, company ensures that the product process optimization is done to satisfy the expectations of customers of quality of packaging, assembly, printing etc. and all other specifications of the good.  Using protective packaging, ensuring no losses during shipment would enable the quality to be maintained (Muhammad et al., 2019).  Having the quality control measures in place and following the processes according to four components i.e., quality planning, control, assurance and improvement, may help the company to identify any issues before the product reaches the customer. This way, the customer’s value of quality will be satisfied.  Customization   Customization of product may impact the operations of company as additional planning and scheduling is required so all the specific requirements of customers are met. Moreover, the inventory management may need to be adjusted for all the components needed to customize products to be available. Thus, by tailoring to the needs of customers, businesses may create value for customers.  Novelty The novelty or creation of new product is linked to the stage of operations as product development, production and components would all need to be changed. In order to satisfy this value of customer, business may need to add new processes in order to change their product to unique requirements thus  new assembly lines or processes or new equipment may be needed. By thoroughly planning the production process of new product, the novelty value of customers may be met through maintenance of company’s profitability. Wellness Customers are seeking wellness in their life and all their activities. In the operations stage, the businesses inputs, processes may need to be changed to incorporate new practices basing on health and wellbeing. For instance, organic ingredients may be used or environmentally friendly production may be incorporated to satisfy demand of wellness conscious customers. Sustainability In order to be more sustainable and meet customers’ requirements, company needs to adapt environmentally and ecologically friendly techniques or production processes. This may be done in form of energy consumption reduction, waste recycling, water saving systems, using renewables sources etc. Outbound logistics Quality Quality is crucial at this stage of value chain in order to make sure the distribution of products to the customer and processes during transpiration and storage all preserve the quality and therefore the customer relationship with the brand is upheld.  The combination of several elements  including  correct storing, distribution, fast processing is crucial for  making up great customer experience and engagement with the company.  Convenience  For the convenience value of customers to be met, businesses need to optimize their warehouse management and transportation management systems. For example, real time product tracking may enable the company to transport the product from warehouse to customer quickly and process the order promptly, satisfying their wants. Moreover, flexible deliver e.g., same or next day, easy returns etc. may be offered to make the shopping more convenient. Marketing and sales Quality The quality of product consists not only of what makes up the product, but rather is a combination of different characteristics resulting from different stages of value chain, including the marketing and sales activities. This stage of value chain influences customers brand image and perception of the company and therefore the product.  Customer value of quality may be satisfied at this stage t hrough effective marketing,  whereby the features such as durability, reliability, serviceability etc. are highlighted as well certifications such as ISO illustrating the company’s commitment to upholding quality standards, are highlighted. By executing this stage well, and having customers as brands advocates, company may be on the road to increase lifetime value of customers (Patterson, 2007), build loyalty and have more positive word of mouth. Info rmation  availability Marketing and sales stage of value chain creation is related to information availability value as the marketing activities enable the company to inform the customer about their company, product, brand, promotions etc. Without marketing, company can portray some crucial information for the customer relating to the company or its production. After sale  service Customers value the after-sale service and thus it has a direct relation to their satisfaction and brand loyalty. For a business, by providing excellent after sale service, their reputation may be enhanced. The sales stage of the value chain is related to this at which it has to be ensured that warranty, possible repair service and information about customer support is provided.  Customization Marketing helps customers receive  the  products which tailors to their needs through different aspects  such as personalized marketing. Through this, specific analytics are used to make  advertisement messages unique to every customer e.g., personalized content recommendations, location-based marketing, personalized landing page  etc.  ( Petterson ,  2022).  Sensory values During marketing, companies may emphasize on the sensory values of their products. This can be done in various ways, for instance illustrating a beautiful aroma after applying a fragrance or illustrating pictures of visually appealing products. Through this, value is created for customers. Entertainment   Marketing stage may enable the entertainment value to be met by understanding what prompts the customer to the company. Through this market research, marketing campaigns may be designed in a way which includes all the devices and platforms which are used by customer. Thus, the omni channel strategy and cross device marketing strategy may be used at this stage.  Social media Social media  is integrated into marketing and sales stage of value chain as it enables engagement with customers. Digital marketing and sales channels allow customers to become aware of a brand and product, ask questions and become active consumers who interact with different touch points.  Self-identity Self-identity value is crucial for customers and powerful marketing tools may place greater emphasis on it as the customers may connect with the brand on a more personal level. Through personal marketing and suggesting products which align with customer’s identity and therefore their beliefs and values, company may benefit from strong relations. For instance, if a customer focuses on sustainability, emphasizing on eco-friendly practices in marketing activities will be beneficial for a company.  Social meaning By emphasizing on social meaning of customer and their conspicuous consumption, it may be a powerful marketing tool for companies as they may create products and promote them in a way which would display their social status and be seen for exclusivity.  Luxury brands often do this by promoting their products through high end marketing campaigns and linking prestige to product. Service A fter  sale  service Providing after sale service in this stage of value chain ensures that customers are satisfied with their purchase and brand and enables company to build a trusting relationship. Through high quality after sale service would ensure repeat business and result in positive word of mouth.  Risks Companies may address the customers value for risk aversion by providing thought out and concrete customer service and being responsive and helpful. This may include some troubleshooting of digital products and devices, refunding, exchanging goods for customers or resolving any other issues on time for customer to be satisfied. Secondary activities:  Firm infrastructure Quality It is an important stage of value chain and thus quality  of activities of management  i.e.  planning, financing, accounting  etc.  is crucial. With effective dealing of these activities, quality products may be produced, enabling customers to be satisfied.  Technology development Customization  Technological development stage involves the development of new technologies in form of equipment, software  etc.  which would enable the customization of a product.  For example, t he 3 D scanning enables many companies now to adjust the garments according to the customization options and enable it to fit well for the customer (McKinsey, 2013).  Novelty New products development and innovativeness requires significant time and resources of companies. The technology development provides the necessary infrastructure for this to be done. Appropriate equipment e.g., 3d printing, software such as Artificial Intelligence and other technologies may be used to create advanced features of a product for customer.  Human resource development Authenticity Authenticity is related to this stage as it is a key factor for creation of positive work culture for the employees and thus in turn enhance the quality of products, they product.  An  authentic company culture would encourage honesty and open communication, leading to high quality service and support for the customers.  Wellness The value of wellness is important for customers as they wish to know that the company prioritizes the health of their employees by fostering a positive company culture. This includes no immoral practices such as child  labor  to be employed.  Psychological costs The psychological costs may be minimized by companies in human resource by investing into the development and training programs for their employees. With this, they will be able to handle customers’ needs and support them in increasing convenience and save time, thereby reducing their psychological costs . Procurement Quality  Customers wish to see high quality of products. Thus, it is crucial for companies to make sure at this stage in value chain that the suppliers provide high quality material which is strong, durable and damage resistance.  Variety Variety allows the company to obtain greater range of material from different suppliers, leading to more flexibility and improved quality. If a company has more suppliers, they may reduce the risks related to their supply chain and provide customers high quality goods at different price points. Economic costs In the procurement stage , a firm can reduce the cost of sourcing materials and resources. This may be done by buying from low-cost suppliers or negotiating the terms. With the reduction in cost for the company, this benefit may be translated to the customer in form of lower price and their economic cost value will be fulfilled. 1.3 Customer values changes:  K ey trends and reasons Having elucidated the types of values,  and how they are related to the stages of value chain of M. Porter, it  is now possible to  shed light on  the key value  trends and the reasons for their popularity.  They are  categorized  according to the typology presented previously.  Functional value s : Increasing demand for quality Customer’s priorities change over time. Previously, the customer's main focus was on the price of the product. However, lately they prioritize quality more , and wish to achieve it for the same or low price. The buying behavior of consumers is rather pessimistic nowadays, as they wish to buy less and focus on quantity. “If we are going to buy less, let it be better”  (HBR, 2014).   A reason for this is an increase in integrity purchases, whereby the customers assess the importance for the society as well, not just the impact on their personal status.  Customers are more focused on investment buying and choosing long-lasting products; therefore, apparel businesses are stressing the investment value of their clothing. Companies are therefore focusing on this fundamental shift in customer’s values and capitalizing on this opportunity. The c ustomers are looking for different quality dimensions in each product and service and thus rank brands objectively on individual aspects of their performance. Although Apple’s products are more expensive than  its  competitors, their sales have been quite remarkable with over $394 billio n  in revenue for 2022  (Macrotrends, 2022)  and there are 1.5 billion user s (Ruby, 2022)  of iPhones in 2023. This illustrates that with customer’s love for the quality of products it develops customer loyalty  and brand image, as according to the Gucci family slogan ‘Quality is remembered long after the price is forgotten’. Growing need for variety The trend for increasing variety has been  instigated particularly due to the online shopping, as it encompasses not only the products variety, but also the channels. Customers nowadays are increasingly looking for seamless options when shopping and prefer a variety of fulfillment options, especially when purchasing online; with omnichannel options such as pick up in store or on curbside and home delivery growing in importance. 83% of customers expect companies to provide flexible shipping options e.g., BOPIS (buy online, pick-up in store), curbside etc. ( Simpson et al. ,  2021). This  value has become a crucial trend as it enables the customers to find the best product, at the most optimal price and at utmost convenience in a certain occasion.  Increasing popularity of customization The customer’s expectations have changed from 20 th  century considerably and p ersonalization matters more to customers nowadays than ever before. With the COVID-19 pandemic, approximately 75% of customers tried new shopping behavior and more than half of them wished to continue with them. This was largely due to the escalation of customer expectations as they noticed personalization practices of leading e-commerce brands. It is not a want, rather a need for customers as they expect to have personalized interactions. 71% of consumers expect companies to provide personalization and 76% tend to get irritated if they don't get it (McKinsey, 2021).   Research by McKinsey indicated that customers wish to receive positive experiences and associate customization with being made to feel special. The aspects of customizations impact potential clients' decision to buy for the first time significantly, some of which are illustrated in the Fi g 1. 3.1 . Fig 1. 3.1 :   Personalization actions influencing customers first time purchase Source:  McKinsey Consumer Survey ‘Next in  Personalization  2021’ It  can be seen that  being  easy to navigate, recommendations about relevant products and tailor ing of  messaging to match the ir  needs are crucial for customers. 76% of customers are more likely to purchase from a company which meets their needs and 78% are more likely to repeat their purchase as well as recommend the company to family and friends. More than 88% of customers prioritize personalization as much or more than 2-3 years prior (McKinsey, 2021). Therefore, personalization impacts the buying behavior on all parts of the customer life cycle  and is an important trend. Seeking  convenience and simplicity As people are making time management a priority, and spending quality time with friends and family has been a priority for many since the pandemic; the convenience and simplicity, therefore economy of time is in the forefront. Consequently, speed is an important element of positive customer experience. This includes instant service and delivery. PwC’s Global Consumer Insights Survey  (2023)  highlights that more than 40% of respondents are willing to pay additionally for same-day delivery.  Consumers generally have busier schedule now than ever before. About 1/3 have less time now than 5 years before for reasons including commuting, certain family responsibilities etc.  (Bhattacharyya, 2022).  It is also interesting to note that the importance of convenience is seen mostly once it is absent. Thereby, customers are inclined to select a brand which guarantees them convenience, and according to statistics, more than 90%   are inclined to this.  Demanding increased information The shutting down of physical stores with the implementation of social distancing in the pandemic led to many customers trying new brands while browsing online. During this search, they value complete information about product and brand more than ever (Periscope, 2020). While surveying, the importance of several factors highlighted in Fig  1 .3.2   grew during the pandemic.  Fig 1. 3.2 :   Percentage of customers citing importance of website features (March vs June 2020) Source:   Periscope by McKinsey (2020)  The customers' need for information about product description and image during the time when they did not have the opportunity to see it physically or try and test it physically ranked it as one of the most vital factors in their online shopping experience.  Therefore,  we can see the increase of its need from 43% to 56% in a span of 4 months during the pandemic.   Moreover,  customers are improving their knowledge about brand sustainability and the practices which may impact the environment and society as a whole before choosing the brand to purchase from and support through their actions. Focus ing on  a fter-sale and support service Customers are using after-sale service as a way of brand differentiation. It is a critical point in building strong relationship between customer and company. As per an article in Harvard Business Review  (1983) , ‘After the sale is over’ after-sale service is of significant importance in value creation for customers and plays a role in the customer loyalty and long-lasting relationship. Hence, companies are investing heavily in this to capitalize on this trend and add additional value for customers.  Experiential value s : Desir ing  greater s ensory  values fulfillment With the digitalization and e-commerce on a rise, and 2.14 billion  ( Rajnerowicz , 2022)  people shop online, customers have had a sensory revolution for customers. The digital transformation has brought changes in sensorial experiences for every sense, for example the touch has been reconstructed with introduction of interactive images whereby the images react to the touch e.g., clothes may ripple according to it.  It was further aided with personalization of marketing efforts for customer to receive improved user experience, positively impacting the senses. Expanding e ntertainment  economy  In 2021, with pandemic all around, global entertainment and media revenue rose by 10.4% (PwC, 2022). The lockdowns increased customers' interest in digital content and companies provided them with exercise  programs , streaming services and online shopping. Young population’s attention has been captured by new platforms, for instance  TikTok , where addictive scrolling keeps 1 billion monthly active users entertained. 66% of consumers find short videos as particularly engaging on social media ( Barhnhart , 2022), thus making it an important part of the entertainment as customer value.  Additional element contributing to the satisfaction of customers' values with social media is the metaverse. Metaverse is the combination of the two: prefix “meta”  i.e.,  transcending and the word “universe”, which represents a hypothetical synthetic environment  which is r elated  to the physical world .  It is a virtual reality which combines physical and digital environments, aided by the junction of the Internet and extended reality (XR). Due to the emergence of different technologies, the virtual world (also known as digital twins) is developing and becoming more integrated into customers' everyday lives with the use of augmented reality (AR), virtual reality (VR) driven human and internet of things (IoT) interactions and connected vehicles. 10%   customers are already engaging with metaverse through the  above-mentioned  means (EY, 2022).  These virtual worlds offer customers the ability to relax, meet new people and solve their problems, all while providing entertainment.  Seeking  increased novelty and innovations People have a tendency to like new things, as the brain’s reward system is activated with each novelty effect. As more dopamine is released, the stronger this effect. Positive amazement as a reaction to novelty allows customers to create emotional engagement with the brand and stimulate  them to make a purchase. Customers nowadays are accustomed to rich content and now are more demanding than ever of immersive information in the digitalized environment. The novelty is a need, rather than a want, and consumers wish to interact with new propositions.  Customers wish to be offered new products and see more creative ‘purple cows’ as Seth Godin writes in his book.  Emphasizing  wellness The customers are more concentrated on their wellness due a number of reasons. Firstly, there is an increased awareness and talks about importance of it. Social media has highlighted the importance of taking care of the physical and mental self  with invoking the people to boost their overall health with a ‘rest and restore’ mode. Secondly, the COVID-19, more customers were becoming conscious of the need to be healthy. Both these factors resulted in a rise in wellness industry over last few years, causing some buzzy wellness trends to prevail: clean beauty products which reduces negative environmental impact, more immunity boosting treatments demanded and minimalism taking the center stage (Zenoti, 2021).  Search ing  for a uthenticity As customers are becoming more conscious about what and from which company to buy, the brand trust has been eroding for the past few years as they fail to provide them authenticity. Back in the days brand trust was associated with a good product at an appropriate price, however now 64% of customers are belief-driven (Edelman Trust Barometer, 2020). Their belief is that brands have the power to change things and purchase f ro m the ones who take a stand. Millennials are on a relentless search for authenticity and wish to receive the ‘real’ instead of ‘perfect’. This was seen from the success of Dove campaign for Real Beauty, which was voted the best US campaign of last 20 years.  The trend is similarly supported by Gen Z, making purchase decisions basing on the support a brand shows for social causes.  The Gen Z along with Millennials wish to hear about the experiences from other clients of the company, which makes user generated content more valued. Prioritizing s ustainability As  customers  become more environmentally conscious, their purchasing decisions are influenced by the company's commitment to environmental sustainability (Ashton et al., 2017). NielsenIQ’s corporate social responsibility survey showed that 55% of global respondents are willing to pay extra for products and services from brands which are committed to positive social and  environmental impact (NielsenIQ, 2014). The purpose driven consumers, who rank the brands which align with their own values higher, are gaining the biggest share of the consumer segment across all products. Thus, as consumers start thinking more about the impact they make on the environment, the more actions they take to support sustainability. Gen Z and  Millennials  are particularly active in changing their daily habits for this purpose (IBM, 2022 ). For  these purpose driven consumers, brands are developing their practices. For instance, Xi Chen, the founder and designer of ‘Sonderlier’, a sustainable fashion brand, shared that the company’s research on customer’s wants for clothing showed that they desire consciously designed clothing which enables them to have a smaller wardrobe without surrendering the variety. (Wertz, 2021). Therefore, by doing just that and creating a minimal capsule wardrobe, the company helps customers and builds brand trust.  Increasing popularity of s ocial media   P w C’s Total Retail Survey of 2016 indicated that 78% of the globally surveyed customers were influenced by social media, and for 45% reviews and feedback left by other customers influenced their behavior while shopping  online  (James and  Skelley , 2016).  Fig  1. 3 . 3  portrays  some of  the aspects of social media which influence this customer's behavior online , and therefore are the major cause of this trend.  Promotional offerings, information, advertisements viewing and being in the know of the fashion trends are some of them.  Thus, it may be said that SNS offers customers 2 different types of information: commercial or market generated and  noncommercial  or user generated content ( e.g.,  word of mouth)  ( Kol  and Levy, 2022).  The customers inside the social media network feel a sense of belonging and are encouraged to engage , therefore increasing their use.  By being in the know, they have access to current trends, personalized experience and clear information, all adding value to customers.  Fi g 1. 3.3 :  Aspects of social media influencing customer’s online shopping behavior Source:  PwC (2016)   Symbolic value s : Emphasizing wellness In recent years, self-identity has become more crucial for customers for several reasons. Firstly, there are changes in consumer’s nature. With more empowerment and being informed and in the loop, people are not just looking at the functional aspects of products and services, but also the emotional and social benefits which they provide. This has led to a belief that the brands may help in the defining and expressing of consumer identities as far more emphasis is placed on the self-expression. Moreover, the proliferation of new subcultures and exclusiveness in communities has created unique values, for whom brands act as signals of membership. The growing use of social media, in addition, helps these consumers express themselves and curate online identities.  Consumer brand relationship has thus been increasingly popular, with brands using anthropomorphism for brand positioning creating positive reactions and nurturing emotional attachment (Delbaere et al., 2011).  Searching for authenticity In this day and age, possessions play a symbolic role in consumers lives. Consumers are craving to satisfy their need for status and brands are becoming instruments for this ‘status signaling’ (Han et al., 2010).  People are increasingly using brands to reinforce or in a way supplement their identity with brands. The trend has been in place for years, but with the global shift in consumer values, the trend has been more focused on brands putting integrity before profit and brand consistency plays an important role in their revenue. Transparency, being vocal about social issues, excellent  customer service and authenticity all play a big part in the choice of customers and whether they choose to buy from a certain brand.  Cost value s :  Focusing on economic costs Similar   to other trends in consumer values,  increased focus on  economic cost has shown a positive trend. According to recent research in 2023, consumers are now  placing  price of the product  on  the highest  place when considering a  purchase , with quality being on second place  (First Insight, 2023).   The Fig  1. 3.4   i llustrates how for customers instore and online, price and deals are the largest influence.  With the age of online shopping, customers primarily filter products based on the price. This was essentially important during the pandemic and crisis when people resorted to stricter budgets.  As per the prospect theory of Tversky and Kahneman, people are risk averse when choosing between gains and pursue it when choosing between losses. Therefore, in times of the crisis, consumers would choose loss aversion and decide  in favour of gains.  Fig 1. 3.4 :  What influences the online and in store purchases Source:   First Insight  ( 2023 ) Minimizing psychological costs   The convenience-oriented customers are now spending more money on time, not physical things. After the global recession and now the pandemic, customers are more dependent on connectivity and omnichannel so as to receive convenience. The main stress is on the importance of  making lives easier and time saving is done in every way possible. Thus, shopping and e-commerce have gained popularity as it makes things done fast and easy.  Mitigating risks Aforementioned trends’ causes have been linked to the recent COVID-19. The risk taking for consumers is therefore also linked closely to this. Health and safety were the foremost priority for people and customer experience had to be leveraged for this. With it, quick contactless operations became a new norm with adoption of contactless payment the preferred method of payment. Thus, gone are the times of high touch commerce and reliance on in store shopping, as people wish to reduce any risk for themselves and  their family. Therefore, we have seen the key trends in customer values and the reasons due to which they are popular among customers, categorized according to the typology used in this paper. They have been summarized in the table 1.4.1 further on in this chapter. Some of the key trends highlighted here are increasing demand for quality, need for variety, seeking of novelty and innovations, prioritizing sustainability, increasing popularity of social media and focusing on economic costs. In the next paragraph, the relation to these values of pandemic will be assessed to see how customers of fashion companies changed their preferences and shopping habits. 1. 4 COVID-19’s influence on  c ustomer  v alues   The aim of  this part  is to examine the influence of COVID-19 pandemic, claimed as one of the biggest crises of the century, on the customers’ values.  As the pandemic spread, the creative fashion industry was hit hard as brick-and-mortar stores closed and people were implementing social distancing, leading to a momentary dip in the value and of the sales of clothing. The problems arose not only in form of internal issues for the companies who were producing the apparel but also externally as consumers values and behaviors were changing. For the majority of  customers,   the change in their values had a direct impact on their purchasing decisions and likewise their approach to and definition of fashion. Therefore, it is important to also consider what impact did the COVID-19 have on the   customers and  in turn t heir values  which were  discussed before. The coronavirus ( COVID-19 ), an infectious disease, caused a global health crisis as of March 2020, led to a halt in economic activities and redefined the word ‘normal’ for people globally (Fernandes, 2020).  It threatened the physical, as well as mental, health of individuals and the overall  quality of life (Ammar et al., 2020). Therefore, it is important to consider it as a turning point in the consumer behavior and consider the impact of it on individuals before going further. In order to do this,  the framework by Smith and Colgate was  implemented to  analyze how the  different customer values  trends were related to or impacted by the COVID-19 .   Functional values: Increasing demand for quality With the pandemic, customers focus  shifted  more on the quality of the product s,  services , experience and time  as the economic uncertainty and hardships which came along with the crisis made customers vary of spending their money on items which fall short of fine quality . They are now increasingly prioritizing how and when they use their money and time, on which products and experiences. Moreover, they wish to consume less overall and 59%   think carefully about how to spend their money  (EY,2021).  The time spent home meant that people realized they own more stuff that they need and wished to declutter their space and in future avoid spending on unnecessary items.  Growing need for variety At the peak of pandemic, consumers priorities centered on the basic needs and categories of essential goods, while the non-essentials slumped, caused by a limited search for variety of the latter category. Moreover, d ue to the extreme social distancing and containment measures,  and to reduce the risk of infection,  many customers moved to the use of new shopping methods such as online shopping, home deliveries and contactless payment, which they perhaps didn’t consider using before (Pantano et al., 2020).  Additionally, w ith the disruption in the global supply chains, customers were encouraged to purchase locally produced and manufactured goods. The interest in purchasing of domestic products was catalyzed in such a way that custome rs felt the need to be self-reliant along with the growing consumer ethnocentrism in many countries (Verma & Naveen, 2021).  Increasing popularity of c ustomizatio n   As customers are spending more time at home and focusing on their individual preferences, t he pandemic has led to an increased demand of customization , as they wish to have a personalized experience in their online shopping journey. The acceleration of e-commerce served as the catalyst for customers demanding hyper-personalized guest experience at all the touchpoints. Digital transformation technologies in the likes of AI, IoT, AR and VR have made this successful ly available  for customers through their smartphones (Forbes, 2022). As per PwC  2018  study, 1/3 customers would not continue their purchase if they are not satisfied with their customer experience. From the product’s customization point, s ome retailers  enable d customers  to choose  the  product  based on what it is made of  certain instances and opt for more sustainable and ethically produced goods  e.g., Patagonia which uses recycled materials in their  clothes . S eeking c onvenience and simpli city McKinsey  report illustrated that in 2021 , the e-commerce and digital platforms  growth had been more than 30% in comparison to 2019,  as the customers shifted to online.  The primary driver of the growth were mostly digital channels used by people as they were reluctant to be in crowded areas for their fear of catching the virus.  The total online spending jumped $52 billion and was up 77% YOY (Forbes, 2020). For health and simplification purposes, consumers portrayed an increased demand for contactless payment. Along with it, QR code contactless ordering was stronger than ever. Juniper Research  (2022)  predicts that by 2024, QR code payments will account for 27% of all the digital commerce transactions.  Last but not the least, consumers increasingly turned to virtual services in forms of email, live chats, social media and websites, seeking faster and convenient service. Demanding increased information As mentioned earlier  with the Periscope survey, customers demanded more information about products in the new digital environment.  The forced closure of physical stores meant that customers relied on their online shopping experiences, resulting in more demand for information such as product safety, the availability, make, and in certain cases such as with apparel the 3D or virtual reality experience in stores. They also paid more attention to the reviews and other customer’s opinions to make sure they are buying the product which is suitable for them.  Focus ing on  a fter-sale and support service 23%   of surveyed customers were not satisfied with the after-sale service experience and wished to see better delivery times, rural areas delivery, and active phone number or chat for the after-sales service problems (McKinsey, 2021).   The companies  there  innovat ed  their  end-to-end  consumer experience by improving their digital availability of products and focusing on the  after-sale  services  through digital tools to reduce in person interactions.  As the companies realized the importance of the return policy, they updated theirs to be more generous as it  provided a source of comfort for people  who are new to the business and did not have guarantees about quality of product.  Retailers such as John Lewis and Sainsbury’s allowed customers to return items once stores reopened, leading to positive brand image. Experiential values: Desir ing  greater s ensory  values  fulfillment Due to the COVID-19, everyone went through a sensory revolution as the context in which people sense was altered (Smith, 2020). As an increasing proportion of the population make their purchases via Internet or online, they are in a way isolated from a total multisensory product experience. Therefore, customers valued new technologies such as Internet of Things, Artificial Intelligence, social media and others to develop a multisensory experience (Platon, 2021). Using these digital interactive technologies, the customers wish to be now be informed about sensory properties of a product e.g., the texture, true color etc.  Expanding e ntertainment  economy In order to deal with isolation times, consumers were using the internet and social media for connecting, learning, playing  etc. , and are likely to continue these habits  (Eger et al., 2021).  Adding on to the previous information about ways COVID-19 impacted customers and their entertainment, several activities can be highlighted. Firstly, with the movie theatres and other physical places closed, customers turned to streaming services. Just in 2 weeks since beginning of pandemic, the time spent on these sites grew by 34% and 156.1 billion people were watching shows online (The Hollywood Reporter, 2020).  Secondly, virtual events were on rise, up to 1000%  (Forbes, 2020)  since the outbreak. Thus, they acted as essential tools for businesses and employees to engage with customers. Lastly, gaming emerged as one of the popular activities for customers. The global increase in time spent gaming was 39%, as per Statista  (2022) .  From the point of retailers, several services were provided to keep the customers satisfied  and entertained , as shown in Fi g 1. 4 .1: Fig 1. 4 .1 :  Key services introduced by retailers for interaction with customers Source:   McKinsey and Company (2020) Seeking  increased novelty and innovations  With the pandemic, the trend of wellness and health has been accelerating. People are more focused on their health than prior to the COVID-19. As a result, they have been increasing their knowledge of what they eat and what goes into the products they use and consume, so they are expecting more from companies, in terms of providing value to their physical, as well as mental, health. The consumers are addressing stress and anxiety more, leading to a greater focus on role of nutrition and a healthier lifestyle. Moreover, the pandemic triggered 25% increase in the anxiety and depression worldwide (WHO, 2022). As a result, the products targeting mental health and support have been increasing in importance. Emphasizing wellness COVID-19 illustrated an increase in consumption of nutrition and medical products. As studies pointed out, healthy and fit people were less likely to get the virus in a very severe form, which has led to shift towards a healthier lifestyle (O’Connor, 2020).
