As a result, they have been increasing their knowledge of what they eat and what goes into the products they use and consume, so they are expecting more from companies, in terms of providing  value to their physical, as well as mental, health. The consumers are addressing stress and anxiety more, leading to a greater focus on role of nutrition and a healthier lifestyle. Moreover, the pandemic triggered 25% increase in the anxiety and depression worldwide (WHO, 2022). As a result, the products targeting mental health and support have been increasing in importance. Search ing  for a uthenticity In times of uncertainty, the authenticity factor became important for customers aspiration.  Stories with genuineness and sincerity were and are likely to be valued dimension of brands personality (Napoli et al., 2014).  The online shopping has made customers more cautious as to which products they buy and wish to have trust in the brands they buy from and authenticity is a way to build it. 60% of customers surveyed said that they are more loyal to brands they could trust (Edelman, 2020). Secondly, pandemic also brought about the ethical concern regarding the employee job security and environmental impact. Thus, customers supported brands which prioritized these aspects more and were transparent about them.  Prioritizing s ustainability   Before the pandemic, customers were in a way overloaded with choice and their consumption was driven by pursuit of products which could meet their higher social and self-actualization needs (Maslow, 1943). However, with the onset of COVID-19, the customers were faced with a situation where their basic necessities needs may not be met.  This led to the reassessment of consumption and a shift to be more responsible and prosocial with it. Therefore, consumers self-identity was more strongly linked to their responsible consumption  (He & Harris, 2020).  In 12 months since the COVID-19 started, 75%  (Deloitte, 2021)  of customers started recycling and composting their waste.  It is also seen in the type of products they buy- in the essential categories  which are  bought often (e.g., household items and beauty products) consumers are more likely to make sustainable and ethical choices   (Deloitte, 2022). Thus, the customers are valuing sustainable practices in companies who: Produce sustainable products Reduce waste in manufacturing Commit to ethical working practices Reduce their carbon footprint Respect human rights Increasing popularity of s ocial  m edia The interaction amongst customers and with businesses was increasingly reliant on social media  due to limited physical interactions. 55%   of people were interested in reading and getting information about COVID-19 from the platforms (Statista, 2023). For customers in particular, it was and is an essential platform to learn about companies and brands. During the pandemic, about 43% of customers discovered new products, services and companies through the use of social. Moreover, 68%  (Cover, 2021)  of consumers used the social media to interact with companies by viewing their posts and treat. It as a feedback loop through which they can report any issues they faced with the product, give a review or send a query to the company.  Symbolic values: Emphasizing self-identity The pandemic had a significant impact on self-identity of customers. To start with, the work was a major component of identity before pandemic, which was disrupted due to changes in work patterns and work from home. About 45% of employees said that the interaction with people at work decreased due to pandemic and 1/3  (Business Today, 2021)  of employees felt disconnected from their work. Additionally, the self-identity also changed due to greater time spent in the confinements of their homes. There was a significant increase in loneliness  and the technologically mediated communication did not prove to be an effective substitute to social interactions (Kovacs et al., 2021). Pandemic also led to increase in focus on health identity and wellness became a key component of identity, with exercise, nutrients and more lifestyle changes integrated by people. Lastly, the coronavirus led to many people feeling a sense of solidarity.  Increas ing  conspicuous consumption With the crisis taking hold, demand for possessions as symbolic items and luxury goods dropped sharply as customer behaviors changed.  The luxury goods market contracted by 23%  as consumer demand for essential goods and action with purpose from brands increased. While many customers did become more cautious about their purchasing decisions and spendings, some continued to purchase them as a form of self-care, known as ‘comfort consumerism’. They took luxury brands as a therapy to help them with anxiety reduction, sensory appeal and motivation (Bain and  Company ,  2020).  Due to their relevance for the consolation of customers, the luxury offerings inspired customers to restore themselves and their wellbeing.  Cost values:  Focusing on economic  costs   Pew Research Center indicated that economic fallout from the pandemic hit the lower income households the most, as they experienced more job losses and the reduced income led to increasing economic costs. The disproportion in economic costs and financial pain across different customer segments can be seen in the Fi g 1. 4 .2 : Fig 1. 4.2:   Financial pain points due to the pandemic across race, ethnicity and income Source:   Pew Research Center (2020) Overall, ¼ adults had troubles paying bills in coronavirus.  The reduced income globally and job losses were another factor of an increased burden of economic costs. International Labor Organization  (2021)  highlighted the magnitude of global employment losses problem of 114 million people being unemployed in 2020. Along with these issues, some customers were high in debt to cover their expenses and the reduced amounts of savings caused an adverse impact on economic costs of customers.  Minimizing p sychological costs  The pandemic has presented consumers with direct and indirect psychological costs. The former was seen in the impact of fear and anxiety of contracting the virus on one’s well-being (Xiong et al., 2020)   due to the stigma and prejudice against the people who got ill. The latter on the other hand illustrated itself on the psychological well-being due to social distancing, lockdowns and quarantines. The disruptions in daily normal and contact with closed ones made them feel socially isolated, having a toll on the mental health (Tang et al., 2020). These changes in turn led to a positive impact on customers’ personal investments in terms of health and well-being, as they invested more in products and services which promoted health e.g., face masks, sanitizers etc.  Mitigating risks D isease cues, in likes of COVID-19, escalate individuals’ perceived risk and act as motivating tools for them to seek more safety and reduce any uncertainty  (Shin & Kang, 2020).   Customers are becoming more risk-averse, and thus avoiding risky purchases, and thus are more prone to buy well-known brands to  avoid any financial risk and are also willing to pay a higher price for the brands they trust (Bao et al., 2003). Moreover, during COVID-19 these risk averse consumers also avoided the risk of purchasing a lower quality product and focused on higher priced brand for the perceived better quality. Sourc e:   Made by author C HAPTER  2:   Pakistan  f ashion  i ndustry:  Key features, market characteristics and business value chain 2.1  The f ashion industry  in Pakistan:  Defining  the  scope and features 2.1.1   Overview of fashion industry Fashion is a dynamic industry, which is a reflection of customer values, preferences and trends. As today’s customers are more informed, connected and discerning, their reaction is more important for brands than ever before. The fast reaction of customers has an immense impact on the fashion industry. As seen before, there are many factors influencing the customers purchasing decisions and key trends in the industry. Therefore, it is important to consider how the influence of customer values in transforming  the industry, driving innovation and creating opportunities and risks for the fashion brands.  Fashion is a method of self-expression revolving around creativity. Fashion industry is a broad term which covers the ‘textile, apparel and footwear’ (Hines & Bruce, 200 7 ) .  It encompasses the ‘design, manufacturing, distribution, marketing, retailing, advertising and promotion of all types of apparel from the most rarefied and expensive haute couture and designer fashion to ordinary everyday clothing’ (Major and Steele, 20 18 ) .  Although it is a vast industry which is concerned with a wide range of goods, some of the main categories it deals with are presented in the Fig.2.1.1 .  From focusing on apparel and footwear, over the years the industry has evolved rapidly and expanded to sectors such as accessories e.g., jewelry and beauty products e.g., perfume ( Djelic  &  Ainamo , 1999)   as well as travel and home goods.  Fig. 2.1.1 .   Fashion Industry (by types of goods) Source:  Made by author   Moreover, it is important to consider the fashion sector pyramid  in  Fig, 2.1.2  which  divides the value creation for customers into segments which range from luxury fashion to low-cost fashion. It illustrates the sector in a pyramid, where the companies at the top produce high-end products with high-margin and low-volume and the ones at the bottom produce low-end, low-margin, high volume products.  Fig 2.1. 2 :   Fashion sector pyramid Source:   HCI in Business, Government and Organizations   ( Sanmiguel  and  Sadaba , 2020 , p.523) This will be taken as the basis  in this  work. First of all, there is a division into luxury and fast fashion. The luxury segment includes  brands which evoke   the exclusivity, have a well-known brand identity and high brand awareness and perceived quality as well as customer loyalty ( Phau  and Prendergast, 200 0 ). The most symbolic and visible aspect of the companies in this segment is their brand name (Okonkwo, 2007). The luxury and prestige brands have traditionally been using premium pricing strategy to highlight the brand exclusivity ( Fionada  and Moore, 2009).   On the other hand, the fast fashion segment is concerned with a more consumer- driven approach as the number of processes of buying cycle are lower along with lead times of new products in the stores, in order to satisfy the consumer demand (Joy et al.,  2012).   Their aim is to react quickly to the changing fashion trends to gain competitive advantage (Barnes and Greenwood, 2006).   These segments are then divided into different categories. Going from the bottom, there are discount or  off-price  market involves dedicated outlets to sell off the products which have not been sold during the season by fast companies. The value market relies on huge sales volume to create profit as the products are usually cheap and the quality is low.  They focus on replaceable, high fashion products.  The top of fast fashion is the mid-market, where products are neither cheap, nor do they classify into luxury due to their low quality. Further on, the premium s the fashion segment in luxury which is above high street and creates a bridge between fast fashion and luxury. They are created by high end fashion houses. Moreover, affordable luxury includes products affiliated with high end brands, which are sold at a lower price than other offerings of the brand. And finally, luxury  includes the most influential fashion brands with exceptional craftsmanship, heritage, scarcity element, strong brand identity and superlative shopping experience.  2.1.2  Key   features  of Pakistan’s  f ashion  i ndustry The fashion industry in Pakistan is growing over time, and the revenue projected for 2023 is USD $ 1,296 million (Statista, 2023). The revenue is expected to grow at 5.14% CAGR from 2023 to 2027. This sector contributes 11% to the GDP and has a role in 60% of the export receipts  ( Cororaton  and Orden, 2008).   It is the biggest industry, with which brands propagate the brands. The markets largest segment is apparel. The cities which hold the top place in the industry are Lahore, Karachi and Islamabad and the industry is led by collectives such as Pakistan Fashion Design Council.  Initially only the high-end customers were catered to by the industry, but with the rise of urban and working class and increase in disposable income, the fashion industry’s customer base increased.  A key characteristic of the industry is the intense competitive rivalry, where the leading companies are using a dynamic approach to adapt to the changing customer values. Moreover, it is important to consider the types of products which are sold in Pakistan by fashion companies as it is different from the usual categories of clothing globally. There are two divisions of the apparels i.e., western and eastern clothing. The western clothing includes jeans, pants, tops, shirts, skirts etc. Whereas, the eastern clothing, or traditional clothing, is based on culture and traditions and includes shalwar kameez (a tunic with trousers), sari (unstitched stretch of fabric worn as a robe), kurtas (collarless shirts) etc. Further on, vibrant colors and embroidery of different kinds is widely used in this type of clothing, as it is deeply enrooted in the history and culture of the people.  This part is important to note as the product and marketing mix of the fashion companies in Pakistan is likely to be different from global ones due to this. Although, lately, the western styles and trends of companies have been integrating into the fashion industry and reshaping the styles. Table  2.2.1.:   Summary of PESTLE-analysis factors Political Economic Balancing, Modernization and Replacement (BMR) program Reduc ed sales tax for companies linked to textile industry Environmental laws  for promoting sustainability Political instability and corruption Concessionary tariff from Pakistan’s Economic Coordination Committee Economic instability and low growth High interest rates High inflation rates Lowering disposable income High unemployment rates Socio-cultural Technological Population growth High  proportion of  working age population Cultural and religious beliefs prevalence    Increasing levels of innovation Inc rease in e-commerce popularity Legal Environmental Prevalence of gender-discrimination Poor  working conditions for blue collar workers Customer protection laws Net Zero Pakistan Environmental policies Increasing sustainability practices Active NGOs participation Source :   Made by author Political  factors Balancing, Modernization and Replacement (BMR) program As apparel industry is  the  fastest growing sector in Pakistan, with annual growth expected to be 5.14% from 2023-2027 and revenue in 2023 is projected to reach approximately 1.3 million USD in 2023 (Statista, 2023), several trade policies and measures have been taken by the government.  The  Balancing, Modernization and Replacement (BMR) program  (Dawn, 2004)  was devised in 2020 to provide financial support in forms of loans to businesses under  concession  rates. This provides the fashion companies loans for a period of 10 years, with 6 months grade period to purchase new machinery for Balancing, Modernization and Replacement of existing units or setting up new units.  This enables new, better quality of apparel to be manufactured, therefore improving the value for customers. Impact on companies As fashion companies in Pakistan require significant specialized machinery for tasks related to production of apparel  e.g.,  cutting and sewing, the efficient equipment is essential for productivity. The BMR program may provide the companies with needed funding for acquiring the equipment and thus improve the quality of garments, reduce the  lead time and overall be a driver for growth and higher sales.  Reduced sales t ax  for companies linked to textile industry Moreover, there has been reduction in the sales tax for fashion companies dealing in textiles by Pakistan’s Federal Board of Revenue  ( Fashionnetwork , 2018) , as they may avail a reduced sales tax of 6% under conditions that they would use integrated system providing full information on sales from all point of sales (POS) through online sales invoices generation. These invoices would enable the fashion companies to gain benefit of lower taxes and therefore use this capital for other purposes such as increasing the innovations and variety of products for customers.   Impact on companies The reduction in the tax would help the companies to reduce their costs and may mean higher possibility to invest in R&D and new opportunities for business growth. However, with the  provision of full information from all POS, the cost incurred may increase as certain resources will have to be used to implement the system. The impact of this would depend on the size of the fashion company.  Environmental laws  for promoting sustainability The government of Pakistan has 6 environmental legislations and a National Environmental Quality Standards, which have to be followed by the fashion industry, including their textile factories, with the aim to protect and conserve the environment. Only 32% of firms in a study were shown to have environmental policies in place (Samad et al., 2015). The larger firms are more likely to adopt these policies, relative to the medium ones and 83% of these large firms adopt them in total quality management. Therefore, in order for the customers value of sustainability to be met, fashion companies need to step up their game and focus more on improving their practices, other than complying with mandatory measures set by the government. Impact on companies The environmental laws may require the companies in Pakistan to adopt sustainable practices  e.g.,  reduce the carbon footprint, use more environmentally friendly materials  etc. , in turn entailing  investment in new technologies and equipment, leading to higher expenses. The expenses may not be as sufficient if looked at in comparison to the benefits this may provide i.e., better brand reputation and competitive advantage, long run benefits of investing in energy efficient equipment and others.  Political  in stability  and corruption One of the biggest obstacles for fashion companies in Pakistan is the political instability and corruption . Last year Pakistan’s rank in corruption perception index decreased further ( Rose , 202 3 ).  As a  result,  there is  uncertainty  in governmental policies about the tax administration and other policies. Due to the inefficient bureaucracy,  a national anti-corruption strategy was drafted, as well as amendments were made to anti money laundering act, all to address this issue and add preventive measures (UNODC, 2021). All these policies make sure that the companies in fashion industry are clean of corruption and the customers value of wellness, authenticity and self-identity by associating with a certain brand they buy from are met. Impact on companies In certain instances, the corruption may lead to bribes or illegal payments to be made by fashion companies necessary to obtain permits or ensure smooth running of the business.  Additionally, with it may come uncertainty in business environment, leading to higher risk of disruptions of supply which may damage the brand reputation.  Concessionary tariff from Pakistan’s Economic Coordination Committee Pakistan’s Economic Coordination Committee implemented a textile and apparel policy, which would provide concessionary tariff to companies on electricity and gas for processing activities, helping reduce the cost of doing business and also attract investments for small and medium firms  (Khan , 2022).  Thus, as the prices may decrease for companies, this benefit may be provided to the customer whose economic cost minimization value will be met.  Impact on companies Pakistan has been facing rising electricity prices along with a record-breaking inflation rate (Reuters, 2022). Thus, the government subsidies would help the companies in reducing cost of production, enabling them to be competitive and maintaining the prices for customers, in turn leading to improved profitability.  Economic  factors Economic  in stability and  low  growth Economic stability of a country is essential for the businesses in fashion sector. Recently, the economy has weakened with lower growth rates of 6%, which is weaker than previously due to the ongoing economic crisis due to weak governance as the external debt surges by 38% ( Livem int , 2023). All over the country an economic depression is witnessed, resulting in reducing purchasing power of customers. Many garment factories have been shut down due to these factors as customers are not willing to pay higher prices and incur more economic and psychological costs.  Impact on companies The reduction in purchasing power of customers may lead to reduced consumer demand as less disposable income would be spent on non-essential items.  However, it may not be the case if there is strong consumer preference for customers, as is the case for Pakistan’s consumers who due to social and/or cultural factors prioritize fashion and style. This also may not be applicable to niche markets and segments.  High i nterest rates In 2023, the interest rate boomed to 21%, which is the highest level since 1992 (Trading economics, 2023).  With the painfully high inflation and increasing demand for lower priced fashion products, there is a crisis in the supply chain. As this also led to decrease in consumer confidence and the self-identity value is at question, the demand for fashion products may reduce when more conscious spending is done in uncertain times. Impact on companies Similarly, high interest rates may also reduce consumer spending as there is less disposable income. Additionally, there may be a decrease in investment or expansion plans as investors may seek to invest in lower risks and high growth options.  High i nflation rates As the economic situation is worsening, the inflation is hitting an all-time 60 year high of 35.4% in March 2023 (Trading Economics, 20 19 ). After the lockdowns and pandemic restrictions, this factor caused a big impact on customers  as the purchasing power and affordability for customers reduces. Moreover, as this also has a toll on businesses they may reduce their production, resulting in lower variety for customers or perhaps lower quality. Impact on companies For fashion companies, the production costs may also inflate as raw materials, labor costs and transportation would rise. The resulting lower profit margins would drive up the prices for  customers. In spite of this, the companies which are well-capitalized, have a strong market presence and image may be better positioned in such a scenario. Lowering d isposable income According to World Bank, as the income level of Pakistan is lower middle, and the disposable income for 2019 was about  41545 PKR  ( Pakistan Bureau of Statistics , 201 9 )  as shown in Fig. 2.2.1.  However, it is important to note that there is high disparity of incomes, with the richest 20%  (United Nations Development Program, 2009)  having the higher average per capita monthly income and this gap between the rich and poor is widening compared to the growth in income by quintiles . Fig 2.2.1.:   Average per capital monthly income  Source:  Pakistan Bureau of Statistics,  20 19 Therefore, the average lower disposable income increases the spending on essential goods and lesser is spent on clothing, accessories and other fashion items. Thus, quality factor is important as one good is bought for long time and economic cost value is important as customers are cautious about their spending. However, the trends among more affluent are different as their disposable income allows them to focus on variety, sustainability, entertainment and other values. Impact on companies The reduction in disposable income may affect supply chain of companies as they would need to find ways to reduce costs leading to possibly impact quality or products or delay production. In certain segments of companies where the main customer is middle income, they may reduce their spendings for a certain time or look for more affordable brands for their capacity.  High  u nemployment rates Low employment is a big challenge for Pakistan’s government. The unemployment rate of 6.40 is projected for year 2023 (Trading Economics, 2022)   due to the economic conditions worsening as stated before. The apparel and textile industry  are  suffering extensively, causing an approximate 10 million textile workers expected to lose job in one province of Pakistan alone   ( Apparel Resources ,  2023).  Impact on companies This may in turn result in reduced availability of variety, higher prices and so higher economic costs, falling quality and problems with sustainability for customers.  Soci o cultural  factors Population growth The population growth of 1.8% in 2021 (World Bank, 2021) has positive implications for the fashion industry in Pakistan as it implies expansion of the market, creating increased sales.  Impact on companies For customers this may translate in increased variety, customization to the needs and innovations. Moreover, there may be greater demand for sustainable practices and more focus on convenience with online shopping. However, the increased population may also bring challenges for fashion companies as there would be higher competition, making it difficult for new entrants and smaller enterprises.  High proportion of working-age population The high percentage of working-age population, 70% in 2021 (World Bank, 2022), illustrates increase in labor force, causing a push up in the labor supply.  Impact on companies For fashion companies this translates into a larger potential pool of customers for them, as more younger customers are likely to be interested in their products.  Moreover, this means that the customer values are evolving e.g., there may be more emphasis on sustainability and convenience.  Cultural  and religious  beliefs prevalence  As Pakistan is an Islamic country, the understanding of religious principles is important for fashion companies to gain a good brand image . The religious festivals also provide opportunities for these companies to adapt their marketing campaigns to target greater audience and earn more profits . Impact on companies As the religion and culture shape customers values and beliefs in Pakistan, fashion companies follow a more modest approach in their eastern clothing. Thus, the eastern clothing such as shalwar kameez being popular for customers of fashion companies means that brands need to incorporate them into the marketing mix, i.e., the product. Technological  factors Increasing l evels of innovation   Leading fashion companies are adopting the newest digital technologies  e.g., in  the manufacturing  processes  in which multi-pass industrial textile printers are used. At the end of supply chain, when product is bought by customer, several technologies are used for promoting and selling it. 360-degree rotations are  starting to be  used to show the product at all angles for the customer, especially during COVID-19 .  Impact on companies With Web 3.0 metaverse fashion technologies, fashion companies have been implementing augmented 3D product view and try on virtual mirrors (Idrees et al., 2020). This leads to customer’s innovation, information availability and convenience values to be fulfilled.  Increase in e-commerce popularity By 2023, the ecommerce market is predicted to reach USD 1,296.3 million. The expected growth rate will be 5.1% (Ecommerce DB, 2022). Recent data shows that half of the transactions are made solely through mobile phones (Business Recorder, 2019).  Impact on companies It means that companies need to implement more technologies to create a more immersive and personalized shopping experience with for e.g., personalized recommendations, virtual try-ons, chatbots and virtual assistants when placing an order on ecommerce page and promoting products on social media. This helps customers to place orders at their convenience, save economic and psychological costs and satisfy their main values. Legal  factors Prevalence of gender-discrimination  The garment sector in Pakistan, is the most important branch in manufacturing industry and employs about 4.672 million people, i.e., a 40% of the total workforces in manufacturing sectors  (The news, 2023).  Therefore, it has a remarkable contribution to job creation. However, the quality of these jobs for  labor  employed is of concern. Firstly, the wages for women remain meagre and there is a gender wage gap across the industry with discrimination. The pay scheme based on piece-rate pay system involves excessive overtime, which results in earnings disadvantage for women as they usually produce lower output ( Akram  and Kashmir, 2015). In 2020, a committee to eliminate discrimination against women was established, the aim of which was to implement gender equality policies to be applied evenly across the country (UNHR, 2020) , however a lot of work is yet to be done for it. Impact on companies The laws against discrimination may lead to higher income and help companies attract more talented workforce. Additionally, the companies which are viewed as supportive of women’s rights may benefit from positive brand image and stronger market position.  Poor working conditions for blue collar workers The labor law in Pakistan states that as per article 37, the employees should have just and humane working conditions and maternity benefits have to be provided to those employed (RIAA Barker Gillette, 2015).  The working conditions of employees in a major part of employment laws.  However, m any employees do not have written contracts, employees work long hours, do not get sick leaves and no maternity leave is provided to them. A case of protests against one of the big players in the industry illustrated unfair dismissal, unsanitary working conditions and lower than minimum wages (Human Rights Watch, 2019). However, the labor inspectorate in different provinces does not implement the labor laws effectively, causing a vicious cycle of poor conditions for employees. Due to this, the customer’s value of focus on wellness or authenticity of the brands comes under question as it is shown to be not followed well. Supervisory systems have been proposed by the governments of different province, which aim to increase the supervision, however the results are yet to be seen. (International Labor Organization, 201 5 ).  Impact on companies The biggest impact may be the reputational damage if reports of these poor working conditions become known to customers, causing the bottom line of brand to suffer. Moreover, violation of the laws could pose certain fines or penalties as well.  Customer protection  laws  As per the UN guidelines on consumer protection (1985) and Consumer Rights Commission of Pakistan (CRCP), consumers have the right to transparency and information, right to choose, to being heard and to be educated. Thus, the customers of fashion companies have the right to product safety, correct advertisement and right to be informed about the product and  its make. Moreover, sale of goods act of 1930 covers the right for customer to receive good quality  and be provided needed warranties. Lastly, electronic transaction ordinance of 2002 covers the protection of customers during online shopping. It sets rules for businesses for electronic payments and signatures. Therefore, customers search for authenticity is covered. Impact on companies The guidelines may lead to fashion companies complying with them to avoid facing legal and financial charges, and thus improve their quality of apparel. Overall, they may have positive impact if companies follow them, causing higher customer loyalty and trust as company delivers.  Environmental  factors Net Zero Pakistan In 20 years from 1998-2018, there were more than 150  extreme  weather events  (Aljazeera, 2020). Thus, a  Net Zero Pakistan   national collaboration  was made  between companies, public institutions and sectoral experts aims to advocate for decarbonization of Pakistan and net carbon emissions. 95% of the signatories of this were from textile sector, as it is the backbone of Pakistan’s economy (Eco textile, 2022) . Impact on companies The fashion companies may be required to reduce their carbon emissions and adopt sustainable practices in lieu with the goal. For this reason, new technologies may need to be bought to reduce energy consumption, or transition to more sustainable raw materials and fabrics. All of these aspects may increase costs of investments and more collaboration with suppliers to produce reduced emissions materials. Environmental  policies The key environmental policies fashion companies have to follow are the following. Environmental Protection Act of 1997 puts forward the guidelines and regulations for environmental protection and defines environmental offences and penalties. Thus, there are approved National Environmental Quality Standards in this act, which fashion companies should abide by. Therein, they must ensure that their manufacturing doesn’t harm the environment. Additionally, c ertain actions have been taken to reduce environmental impact  with these  guidelines . For example, to reduce hazardous chemicals in textile industries, a 43 million USD program   was launched by Government of Pakistan with that of Bangladesh, Indonesia and Vietnam.  This provides the customers with high quality, sustainable garments  (UN environment, 2022). Impact on companies The companies would need to ensure that they correspond and adhere to the regulations, and thus make sure the manufacturing processes do not harm the environment by minimizing use of waste and water, and any hazardous chemicals. The program would enable them to produce higher quality and sustainable garments as they receive support from government.  Increasing s ustainability  practices Recycling is becoming an important issue in Pakistan’s fashion industry.  Used textile is being recycled to divert the textile waste from landfills (Garson and Shaw, 2019).   All these practices enable customers sustainability value to be fulfilled. Moreover, companies are using  postindustrial  and pre-consumer waste to produce fiber which can replace cotton in fabric and thus reduce the carbon footprint of Pakistan  and be more sustainable. Further to that, eco-friendly fabrics are being used as there is a rapid increase in made in green product. Thus, fashion companies are becoming more conscious of sustainable practices and are investing in laser machines and water efficient washing machines to reduce waste (GSP hub, 2020).  Impact on companies By using recycled textiles, companies may save their costs of production as well as reduce amount of waste that goes into the landfills. Moreover, investment in eco-friendly fabrics may help the brands comply with the regulations to reduce risks of penalties.  Active NGO participation Several NGOs in Pakistan have been working to combat the problem of climate change and reduce the impact of fashion industry on it. Firstly, WWF- Pakistan has several programs which are focused on sustainable textile production. They aim to develop and train the top companies with based on Zero Discharge of Hazardous Chemicals (ZDHC) standards, therefore  helping them reduce the degradation of climate (TUV  Rheinland , 2021). Moreover, Fashion Revolution, a global movement advocating for sustainable fashion industry, has a local branch in Pakistan to raise awareness about companies social and environmental impact and also promote sustainable practices.  All these actions enable the customers to get more information about the possible lacks in companies’ practices, urge them to sustainable practices and therefore reduce their own psychological costs.   Impact on companies Working with these NGOs and adhering to the sustainable practices may enhance the company’s reputation as consumers are more aware of the impact of their consumption on ecology.  Moreover, working with WWF on adoption of ZDHC standards could increase innovation of new technologies to reduce harmful chemicals usage.  Therefore, after analyzing the market’s scope and characteristics in depth, it is possible to analyze the prominent fashion companies in Pakistan and their value chain. 2 . 3   Analysis of Pakistan’s  fashion companies  value chain   After analyzing the key features of the market and its characteristics, the fashion companies’ business value chain is now studied by analyzing the stages on basis of the customer values mentioned earlier.   2.3 .1.  Research design and sample selection   In order to carry this out, interviews were arranged and carried out with representatives of fashion companies in Pakistan. The companies which were chosen for this were selected based on the following criteria: Operating in Pakistan Target market is brand aware Primary focus: fashion products i.e., women’s and men’s apparel Private Limited Company Target market: Similar age range and characteristics i.e., income, gender, occupation  The companies whose representatives were interviewed are popular and well placed in Pakistan included the following: By  The  Way (1), Sapphire (2) and Company 3 (which wished to stay private) (3).  By The Way (BTW), founded in 2016, is a retail apparel and fashion company with up to 200 employees. It is a family business owned by Din Industries. The company associates itself with modern street style and focuses on men’s and women’s clothing including eastern and western wear, as well as children’s wear, accessories and more. Thus, the primary target market is Generation Z men and women, as well as children. The value proposition BTW is to  distinguish itself from its competitors, attract new customers, and retain the loyalty of existing ones . The focus is on affordable fashion ; therefore, the target market is  middle and upper middle class, also known as quantile 4. Sapphire Retail Limited (Sapphire), founded in 2014, is a high street apparel brand in Pakistan and employs more than 1000 employees. It is a subsidiary/’sister company’ of Sapphire Textile, which was founded in late 1940s.  Sapphire is a vertically integrated company which started off as a textile manufacturer, producing yarn and fabric and since 2014 has established the company Sapphire Retail producing finished garments.  The company targets customers of all ages: from age 16-60 and thus has a broad range of customers through their different categories: eastern wear and western wear for women, eastern and western wear for men, home and kids. As per the representative, company aims to target quantile 4 and quantile 5 of Pakistan. The former is middle and upper middle class of Pakistan (can afford clothes which cost 10,000 Rs.) and the latter is the upper class (can afford apparel which costs 25,000 Rs.). The company’s competitive advantage consists of their manufacturing facilities: from the raw material until the finished product, they manufacture it all themselves, and thus the costs are minimal. The value proposition of the company at the time of launch was based on providing affordable designer wear and since then has expanded to brand being a “one stop solution” for its customers where they can buy clothes for their whole family as well as home textiles and is working on introducing more categories into this category in the future. “ The overarching theme of the collections is providing affordable designer clothes to the masses.” Company S, which requested to stay anonymous for their privacy, is a denim specializing and producing company based in Pakistan since 2004. It is a part of a bigger group, which is a vertically integrated denim company. The brand is popular in Pakistan for producing western clothing for men, women and children. The company targets the quantile 4, i.e., the upper middle class of Pakistan. It has expanded to other countries such as Dubai and Middle East. Its value proposition is to “offer customers refined denim which is stylish and high quality”. The brand attracts customers through their quality and design.  On average, the interviews were 40 minutes up to an hour in duration and were structured in sections, covering topics: General characteristics of the company General customer profile Changing customer behavior Major value chain  aspects COVID-19  impact on company The first two sections aimed to gather information about the company, identify the competitive position, a general customer profile focused on understanding the segmentation, customer profile and expectations. The third section, aimed to explore how the customer’s behavior has changed and how it has impacted their purchasing decisions. The next part, and the longest, was aimed at  the major  value chain  stages , including marketing ones. CHAPTER 3: Pakistan customer values and fashion companies' value chains: correlation and managerial implications 3 .1   Determining Customer Values in Pakistan’s Fashion Industry     3.1 .1  Research  m ethodology : Understanding Customer Values In order to understand the customers in Pakistan and their values  and see how it is related to the global values highlighted before, a questionnaire was made and distributed. The characteristic of this sample  includes: Gender:  W omen and  M en A ge :  18- 56 years old Employment:  employed have a certain expenditure on clothing and fashion products Income: Different  sources of income e.g., have own business or work in a corporate job.  Thus, this questionnaire aims to preserve the classification of the customer values explained before and the results from these will be considered from this point of view. The questionnaire is presented in the Appendix   B   at the end of the paper. It was distributed to the people  in Pakistan  who are customers of fashion brands and are interested in their products. They include consumers who are actively involved in the customer journey and may be loyal to a certain brand but also may be customers who do not keep up with trends, but for whom the quality and variety is important more than brands. 3 . 1 .2  Key findings about   c ustomer  v alues The distributed questionnaire , for a total sample size of 107 Pakistani customers,  provided  certain  results, which ha ve  been aligned with the customer value typology  for purpose of analysis .  As for the demographic characteristics of the participants, it was different from expected , as can be  seen on Table  2.2.2.   The  majority of the respondents were women, from age 18 to 25 years old , with students predominating, and therefore source of income was partially employment and partly their parents. The monthly planned expenditure on purchase of clothing was divided into two with  32.2% of respondents spending less than expected 5,000 and the same percent spending in the range anticipated. Functional values Firstly ,  qu ality is important to 9 4 . 8 % of the customers. Information about the product was not very significant for the  majority of  customers as only  13.8 % picked it as the most important aspect.
